Some good research on AI use in hiring assessments.

There’s a lot of noise in this space at the moment.

This paper is one of the first to look at what candidates are actually doing in real assessments.

A few findings stood out:

1️⃣ AI use is rising quickly, but still uneven

In 2024, fewer than 3% of candidates reported using GenAI.

By 2025, that number had jumped to over 20% in some groups.

This isn’t a future problem. It’s an adoption curve that’s already started.

2️⃣ Most candidates aren’t “cheating the whole test”

AI use was typically limited to a small number of responses.

The most common reasons were:

◾ clarifying instructions
◾ dealing with cognitively difficult questions

That suggests something more nuanced than wholesale cheating.

It’s often a response to uncertainty and task difficulty.

3️⃣ Cognitive tests are more exposed than personality

Around two-thirds of AI use was on problem-solving questions.

Very little was used on personality measures.

Different assessment methods are not equally vulnerable.

That matters for how we design selection processes.

4️⃣ Simple warnings actually work

A short pre-assessment warning roughly halved reported AI use.

No complex tech. No proctoring.

Just a clear signal about expectations and consequences.

Importantly, there was no negative impact on candidate experience.

Which is exactly why this matters in practice.

At Clevry, we take a layered approach rather than relying on a single control:

- upfront honesty contracts to set expectations
- clear warnings before and during the assessment
- no copy/paste or screenshots
- alerts if candidates click away from the screen
- flagging behaviour that diverges from these parameters

Because the reality is, this isn’t about catching people out.

It’s about reducing temptation, setting norms, and protecting the integrity of the data.

AI in assessment isn’t going away.

But neither is good measurement if you design for it properly.

Credit to Minerva Psychology for bringing this research to my attention.

Most contractors do not have a hiring problem.

They have a starting problem.

The worker shows up. No one is ready for them. Induction done on the fly. 
Contract sorted three days later. Hours tracked on a scrap of paper.

They clock it immediately. And they are gone by week two.

You have seen it. Probably caused it once or twice.

In 2026 it also carries legal risk. No written record means no protection when something goes wrong.

Fix it before the next hire:

• Worker verified before day one
• Contract and hours in writing before they start
• CIS confirmed, not assumed
• Shifts logged from week one
• Apprentice incentives claimed correctly (up to £5,000 available, most SMEs never apply)

One page. Free. Built for UK contractors.

One messy hire in 2026 could cost more than you made on the job. 
Comment "CHECKLIST" before your next hire starts.

*AMERICA SPENT $285 BILLION… AND MAY STILL LOSE THE AI WAR*
A striking paradox is emerging in the global AI race: unprecedented capital, unmatched infrastructure—and a rapidly weakening talent pipeline.
A recent report by Stanford University lays out a reality that should concern policymakers, investors, and technologists alike.

1️⃣ The Collapse of the Talent Magnet
The United States, long the global hub for AI talent, has seen an 89% collapse in net inflow of AI researchers since 2017
Even more alarming: 80% of this decline happened in just the last 12 months
This is the same ecosystem that created the transformer architecture and gave rise to:
OpenAI
Anthropic
Google DeepMind
xAI
👉 Capital is flowing. Talent is not.
2️⃣ The $100,000 Visa Barrier
The cost of hiring foreign AI talent via H-1B visas has reportedly surged to $100,000 per hire
Up from $2,000–$5,000 earlier, this is not a cost increase—it is a structural deterrent
Consider the implications:
A Chinese postdoc from Tsinghua University → $100K entry ticket
A French ML engineer → $100K
An Indian PhD critical to your model → $100K
👉 This isn’t friction. This is a talent blockade.
3️⃣ China Is Closing the Gap—Fast
Performance gap between top US and Chinese models has narrowed to ~2.7 percentage points
Down from 20+ points just two years ago
Since early 2025, leadership has changed hands multiple times
China now leads in:
AI publications
AI patents
Industrial robot deployment
Meanwhile, the US ranks 24th in AI adoption, behind countries like:
United Arab Emirates
Singapore
👉 Innovation without adoption is not dominance.
4️⃣ The Talent Equation Has Flipped
Nearly 50% of the world’s top AI researchers are Chinese, as noted by Jensen Huang
For two decades, the US playbook was simple:
Attract global talent
Retain them with opportunity and incentives
Build the future on imported intelligence
That model is now breaking.
AI labs are quietly expanding outside the US:
Zurich is emerging as a new hub
European and Asian ecosystems are gaining ground
👉 Talent goes where it is welcomed—not where it is restricted.
5️⃣ The Capex Illusion
The US is investing $285+ billion annually in AI
Massive bets on:
Data centers
GPUs (led by companies like NVIDIA)
Cloud contracts
Energy infrastructure
But here’s the uncomfortable truth:
AI is not a hardware race. It is a human capital race.
Without the right minds:
Chips are idle
Data is underutilized
Models stagnate
👉 You cannot compute your way out of a talent deficit.
6️⃣ The Strategic Risk Ahead
If the US loses its AI lead, it won’t be because:
China had better algorithms
Or more capital
Or superior hardware
It will be because:
It restricted the very talent that built its advantage
The loss will not happen in a lab.

The global AI race is entering a new phase:
From capital dominance → talent competition
From innovation → execution and adoption
From openness → strategic control
Nations that understand this shift will lead

I listened to a great conversation with LinkedIn's CMO Jessica Jensen about how to grow your presence on LinkedIn, build your profile and reach, about job searching, the skills employers are looking for right now, and the attention economy. Oh, and what selfies have to do with any of it 🙂

Spoiler: the most essential skill is AI literacy - but don't write your posts with AI 🙂 The LinkedIn algorithm itself can tell the difference. 

Over the past 3 years, the number of posts on LinkedIn has grown by 41% (a portion of them AI-generated). That's 1.3 billion members and 70+ million companies. 
The number of people adding "founder" to their profile has grown by 60%, and 50% of LinkedIn members are currently looking for a new job!

Everyone wants attention, contracts, jobs, clients, sales, and reach - but obviously the numbers are dropping. So what should you do? Especially given that LinkedIn is actively downranking AI-generated posts.

What employers are looking for: AI literacy and capability + skills AI can't replace: negotiation, collaboration, and creativity. So the best thing to have (and keep developing!) is: human creativity, human judgment, strategic thinking, clear writing, clear communication.

3 tips for posting:
- your own point of view, show your values and experience
- short video - don't overthink it, just record something short and add captions
- Yes, personal photos and fun selfies get more reach - Jessica herself once went live wearing flamingo glasses, and it became her most popular post :) 
- Engage in the comments: reply to comments on your own posts AND comment on other people's posts.

LinkedIn job market data for 2026:
- 50% of LinkedIn members are currently looking for a new job
- Hiring has slowed down, yes - but due to macroeconomic factors, not AI
AI is not eating jobs; it's creating more jobs than it destroys
- Younger people more interested in trades (plumbing, electrical work) that AI won't be able to replace.

On the LinkedIn algorithm (nothing new, btw):
- The algorithm is constantly changing. AI-written posts are easy to spot - LinkedIn lowers their priority
- What helps: a unique point of view + engagement + strategic network building
- Gender, race, and demographics are not factored into the algorithm

Useful new LinkedIn features (you may not have known about):
- For job seekers: AI Job Search - you can describe yourself in free-form text, not just a job title, and unlock a broader search. Plus a huge library of learning materials from LinkedIn (AI, marketing, new skills)
- For brands and marketers: Brand Link - advertising alongside top creators; Thought Leadership Ads - promotion through personal content, not corporate; Events platform - 20,000 events per week, great for lead generation

And finally: on the stigma around the "Open to Work" badge - recruiters are actively searching for candidates using it, so use it without fear. But there are ideas to rebrand it as "open to triple your sales," etc. 😄

Wipro has officially announced its biggest-ever buyback of Rs 15,000 Crore! 

This comes at a time when Wipro is navigating its 3rd consecutive year of revenue decline.

Here is everything you need to know about the numbers:

1. Rs 15,000 Crore reward is a tender offer. This means Wipro will buy shares directly back from shareholders at a fixed price.

2. Buyback Price: Rs 250 per share.

3. This price is 19% higher than the market price of Rs 210.20 (as of April 16).

4. Total Shares: Up to 60 crore equity shares, which is about 5.7% of the company's total shares.

By law, 15% of the buyback (Rs 2,250 crore or 9 crore shares) is reserved for small shareholders who hold shares worth less than Rs 2 lakh.

Small investors often use this arbitrage to buy shares at market price and sell them back to Wipro at Rs 250 premium for a quick gain.

While the buyback is great for shareholders, Wipro's core business is facing a weak start to the new year (FY27).

On the other hand, Wipro is moving into AI. They just announced a Rs 590+ crore deal to acquire the client contracts and workforce of Alpha Net.

Alpha Net is a California-based expert in AI-powered software development. By buying their contracts, Wipro shortcuts its way into high-growth AI projects with global clients, helping to stop the revenue slide.

Wipro in Q4 & FY26

1. Q4 Net Profit: Rs 3,501.8 Crore (Down 1.9% YoY)
2. Q4 Revenue: Rs 24,236.3 Crore (Up 7.7% YoY)
3. Cash Conversion: 112.6% of net income
4. FY27 Guidance: -2% to 0%

Wipro is using its cash reserve to support its stock price through the buyback while it tries to reinvent itself as an AI-first company.

A few weeks ago I wrote about my co-founder sharing internal information with someone we were letting go — because a protocol I'd never externalized existed only in my head.

That was one protocol. Turns out almost everything was missing.

So I sat down and mapped every unwritten rule we'd been operating on. 

Here are the seven co-founder protocols I've built for Mo Ads:

1. Information access — who sees what, when. What's open by default vs. need-to-know. What changes once contracts and NDAs are signed.

2. Decision rights — which decisions can each of us make alone, and which require both. Including financial — who can commit to what spend without mutual sign-off.

3. People decisions — who has final call on hiring, firing, and performance conversations. Who communicates the decision. What gets shared with the team, and when.

4. Conflict resolution — what happens when we disagree and neither budges. Without a tiebreaker, the louder person wins or the decision gets avoided entirely. Both are dangerous.

5. Communication cadence — what gets a message, what gets a call, what waits for the weekly sync. This matters even more when co-founders have different working rhythms and energy cycles.

6. Offline protocol — what happens when one of us is unavailable for days. Which decisions pause. What gets documented for review. What absolutely cannot move forward without both of us.

7. Role boundaries — not titles, but actual ownership. Who owns product. Who owns fundraising. Who owns client delivery. When everything is "we do it together," nothing has an owner.

This isn't about legal agreements or equity splits. Those matter, but they're not what breaks startup co-founder relationships day to day. 
What breaks them is the operational layer — the invisible assumptions about how you actually work together.

We didn't have any of this when we started. Not because we were careless — because we were moving fast, building an AI-driven e-commerce agency from scratch, and protocols felt like they'd slow us down.

What I underestimated is how differently two co-founders can operate. It's not about who's right. It's that experience creates assumptions you don't even realize you're carrying.

That's not a communication failure. That's an architecture failure.

Writing these protocols took about two hours. Should have been day one of any early-stage startup. But better late than structural.

I've turned these into a quick one-page co-founder protocol template — not the full operating version, but a starter that helps you aalign on the essentials before things break. You can always expand each protocol into deeper documentation as your startup operations grow.

If that's something you'd actually use — tell me in the comments and I'll share it.

Because trust without structure is just hope.

𝗪𝗵𝗼’𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗴𝗼𝗮𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝗮𝘁?
The word is now in every Investment Committee.
Does this asset have a moat? Will AI erode it? Can we dig a deeper one?
Good question. But hard to answer. Warren Buffett invented the metaphor — but never handed us a concrete framework to analyse defensibility at company level.
Who are the best business thinkers to turn the metaphor into a method? I’d pick two names.
𝗛𝗮𝗺𝗶𝗹𝘁𝗼𝗻 𝗛𝗲𝗹𝗺𝗲𝗿’𝘀 𝟳 𝗣𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿𝘀.
A power exists only when there is both a benefit to the firm and a barrier to imitation. Most “moats” fail the second test.
𝟭. 𝗦𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗲 𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗲𝘀 — unit costs decline with volume. The bigger you get, the harder you are to attack. (Amazon logistics; lab and inspection network density.)
𝟮. 𝗡𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗲𝘀 — value to each user rises with total users. Winner-take-most dynamics. (Visa, LinkedIn; data and benchmarking platforms.)
𝟯. 𝗖𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿-𝗽𝗼𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 — a new business model the incumbent 𝘤𝘢𝘯𝘯𝘰𝘵 copy without damaging its own. The most misunderstood — and the most relevant in AI today. (Netflix vs Blockbuster, Vanguard vs active managers; AI-native delivery models vs legacy bodyshops.)
𝟰. 𝗦𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀 — the cost (financial, procedural, emotional) of leaving. (Core banking systems, ERP; long-cycle audit mandates and embedded FM contracts.)
𝟱. 𝗕𝗿𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 — durable affective value that lets you charge more for the same thing. Rare. Often confused with marketing spend. (Hermès, not Gucci; “nobody gets fired for hiring them” firms.)
𝟲. 𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗰𝗲 — preferential access to an asset others can’t get. Patents, licenses, talent clusters — increasingly, proprietary data. (ASML, Pixar 1995–2005; notified-body accreditations and regulated testing approvals.)
𝟳. 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗽𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿 — embedded know-how that compounds over years and resists imitation. (Toyota Production System; methodology factories and delivery engines.) Also the power AI erodes fastest.
Why the grid wins: it’s a 𝘁𝗲𝘀𝘁, not a checklist. And it’s 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 — a business model can be stress-tested power by power.
𝗢𝗻𝗲 𝗰𝗮𝘃𝗲𝗮𝘁, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗮 𝗯𝗶𝗴 𝗼𝗻𝗲.
Hamilton Helmer tells you if a power holds. He doesn’t tell you if it still matters.
That’s where 𝗔𝗱𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗦𝗹𝘆𝘄𝗼𝘁𝘇𝗸𝘆’𝘀 𝗩𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗲 𝗠𝗶𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 framework is the critical complement. Value migrates. Profit pools move. The deepest moat in the world is worthless if it’s protecting a value pool that’s draining.
The real question isn’t “does this business have a moat?”
It’s “does this business have a moat 𝗮𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗲 𝗽𝗼𝗼𝗹 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗼𝘄?”

Day 40 of #100DaysOfAI
A lawyer spent 3 days reviewing 200 contracts. AI reviewed them in 4 hours — and caught clauses the lawyer missed.

━━━━━
Legal is one of the last professions people expected AI to transform — and one of the first it is actually reshaping.

HSBC announced in January 2026 that it is deploying Harvey AI across its entire legal department. Deutsche Telekom reports Harvey saves each lawyer 5 hours per week. Repsol has 96% adoption across its 200-person legal team.

THE NUMBERS:
· AI is up to 80× faster than lawyers at document analysis — 2025 benchmark
· 5 hours saved per lawyer per week — Deutsche Telekom + Harvey AI
· Harvey's Series E valuation: $5 billion (June 2025). Legal AI is not a niche.

━━━━━

📄 CONTRACT REVIEW AND DUE DILIGENCE
AI reads and extracts provisions from entire deal rooms in hours. Harvey focuses on purchase agreement analysis and compliance flagging. Crosby AI delivers a 58-minute median turnaround on contract review — vs days for manual review.
Proof: 2025 benchmark — AI up to 80× faster than lawyers on document analysis with comparable accuracy.

⚖️ LEGAL RESEARCH — CASE LAW AND REGULATORY
Westlaw Precision with CoCounsel now holds the US federal judiciary contract. Lexis+ AI generates synthesised research memos with source-linked citations.
ABA Formal Opinion 512 (July 2024): lawyers using GenAI remain fully responsible for verifying outputs. AI accelerates research; attorney judgment still required.

🏛️ REGULATORY COMPLIANCE MONITORING
AI monitors regulatory feeds, flags changes affecting operations, drafts preliminary impact assessments. Deutsche Telekom uses Harvey across Legal, Compliance, and Data Protection — including public tenders.
May 2025: UK SRA approved Garfield.Law as the first law firm authorised to deliver legal services entirely through AI.

🌐 AI-NATIVE LAW FIRMS — THE NEW ENTRANTS
NormAI (backed by Blackstone, Nov 2025): Norm Law — "first AI-native full-service law firm for global institutional clients," serving $30T+ AUM.
Google-backed Lawhive acquired UK firm Woodstock Legal Services (Sept 2025) — believed to be the first AI company acquisition of a traditional law firm.
Harvey: used by 50%+ of AmLaw 100 firms, 1,000+ customers in 59 countries.

━━━━━

AI is not replacing lawyers. It is replacing the parts of legal work that should never have required a lawyer's full attention in the first place — the clause-by-clause scan, the preliminary research trawl, the regulatory change log.

What is left when AI handles the volume work is the part that actually requires judgment, strategy, and client trust.

Tomorrow: AI in Human Resources — resume screening, performance management, and the bias questions every HR professional needs to ask about AI hiring tools.

Does your organisation's legal team use AI tools yet? Tell me where they are on this. 👇

The most unexpected part of building an AI-first startup isn’t the technology. It’s the people 😌

We’re actively hiring right now, and HR regularly brings me cases that make me pause and ask: is this serious?😁

Some candidates genuinely complain that we are “taking jobs away from speaker agencies.”
And in those moments, I want to say honestly: yes, because a market with bloated teams and inefficient processes inevitably gets replaced by technology. This isn’t about “taking jobs,” it’s about a model that stopped being effective.

Some expect equity for a short-term 2–3 month contract – as if it’s a default benefit, not a tool for those who actually share the risk.

But the most interesting part starts when you get into conversations about results.

Very often I hear: “I brought $X revenue to the company.”
Then you look at the context – a large corporation, a strong brand, well-established processes, huge teams that execute, support, and de-risk every step.

And this doesn’t diminish the experience. It’s just a different reality.

Because in a startup, you can’t say “I was part of it.”
Everything is on you.

You don’t pass things along – you close them yourself.
You don’t just propose – you deliver results.
You don’t rely on a system – you build it.

And that’s why comparing “I made billions for a company” in a corporation versus in a startup is not the same thing at all.

In a corporation, you have infrastructure, brand, resources, and dozens of people amplifying every move you make.
In a startup, to make the same $3 billion, it’s no longer about your role. It’s about a level of pressure, uncertainty, and responsibility where there is no team of 200 people to catch you if you don’t push it through.

And this is where the gap appears.

Many smart, experienced, high-quality people…
are not ready for a format where there is no “we” to hide behind.

And the people who have actually built revenue from zero in startups are very few.
And almost always, they are not looking for a job – they already have options.

So every time I look at a strong CV, I have only one filter in mind:
how ready is this person to not be part of a system, but to be the system itself?

Founders, what are the strangest or funniest hiring situations you’ve had?

The AI wave isn't just coming for the blue-collar world.

It’s already washing away the foundation of the professional class. If your job involves moving data, interpreting contracts, or managing workflows, you’re in the crosshairs.

I see it everywhere. Coders, accountants, lawyers, and middle management are all being squeezed because the efficiency gains are too massive to ignore. 

If you think a degree or decades of experience will protect you, you’re missing the reality of this shift. We’ve moved from the era of doing to the era of directing.

I’m 70 years old and I’m currently launching two separate web-based startups. 

I’m doing it without hiring a single coder or developer. 

I’m using the tools to build the architecture myself.

The winners in this new economy won’t be the people who perform tasks the best. They’ll be the people who own the outcomes. 

AI is now the ultimate task-performer, which makes the traditional worker a commodity and the architect priceless.

You have to stop being the engine and start being the driver. You need to own these tools before they own your career. 

AI is great at logic, but it’s terrible at navigating human ego, politics, and the messy reality of closing a deal. That’s where the value is now. 

Combine human experience and intuition with AI logic and task abilities.

That’s the new world.

The middle is disappearing. You either become the person who leverages the machine to create massive value, or you get replaced by it.
